summary: DHH defends his characterization of the Canadian trucker protest as 'peaceful' against critics who labeled it violent. He argues that expanding the definition of 'violence' to include hateful rhetoric, honking, or the backgrounds of participants is a deliberate rhetorical strategy to justify disproportionate government responses. He contends that once a protest is labeled violent, it unlocks draconian crackdowns that would be illegitimate against peaceful demonstrators. He criticizes the Canadian government for choosing alienation and persecution over engagement and concession.
key_points: Labeling a protest 'violent' is a rhetorical move that justifies maximum-severity government responses, which is why the designation is so fiercely contested
Expanding the definition of violence to include speech, rhetoric, or the backgrounds of participants conflates distinct concepts to serve a political agenda
Peaceful protest derives its power precisely from denying legitimacy to severe crackdowns — stripping that label away is the first step toward authoritarian response
A healthy democracy would engage with protestors and offer reasonable concessions rather than demonize and persecute them
The Canadian government's punitive response risks radicalizing the very people it seeks to suppress
money_quotes: Once you've claimed something to be violent, you've justified the maximum severity of a response.
Actions that would never seem reasonable against 'peaceful protestors' are now suddenly warranted.
Every despot under the sun knows to legitimize their disproportionate intervention by claiming to protect the security of the people.
If no credible threat to such security exist, they manufacture it, even if just rhetorically.
Disputing that assessment was and always is the key to unlocking a draconian response.
Instead the response appears designed to radicalize further. To alienate. To ostracize. And to persecute.
Violence is violence. Everything else is people shouting.
key_insight: Redefining 'violence' to encompass speech and disruption is not a semantic debate — it's the rhetorical mechanism that legitimizes authoritarian crackdowns on dissent.
